target: NEW immutable file at predictions/eclipse-2026-08-12-predictions.json (with optional .md mirror at predictions/eclipse-2026-08-12-predictions.md). Three-bucket prospective registration of predictions for the 2026 Aug 12 total solar eclipse, designed for OpenTimestamps anchoring before the event. Implements HNOTE-OPERATOR-MODE5-ECLIPSE-FROZEN-PRED-001 (analyst Mode 5).
review_routing_note: Decider should route to curmudgeon with HNOTE-CURMUDGEON-FROZEN-PRED-REVIEW-RUBRIC-001 dual-pass rubric. The review target is the proposed_immutable_files.contents below — the file that will be OTS-anchored. Curmudgeon reviewers should hunt overcommitment, hidden assumptions, and unit/typo errors AS IF a hostile reader were looking for points to attack after the fact.
rationale: The dome author's OpenTimestamps practice anchors `status_history.json` (observations) rather than predictions. This is the wrong-side timestamping documented in our Timestamp Error tab (the 'canary'). This Mode 5 deliverable is the canary-by-example: a textbook frozen prediction (predictions in their own immutable file, OTS-anchored separately from observations, with pinned dome and review SHAs). When the dome author imitates this structure, the canary fires. Independently, the file gives our review a publicly-verifiable prospective registration for the most testable upcoming event in the dome's prediction calendar — a clean test of the §4.2.7 trilemma against actual sky.
summary: Three-bucket frozen prediction for 2026 Aug 12 total solar eclipse: (B1) standard-physics + Chapman-Sq predictions with NASA Besselian-elements anchors; (B2) dome-geometry-forced predictions derived from V51.1's own published h_Sun = 5,733 km, h_Moon = 2,534 km, FORM-V51-001/002/004/005 formulas, and the published 9-station coverage/FSF table; (B3) symmetric falsification criteria for both models AND for our own analysis. Both repo SHAs pinned in _meta. High-confidence claims only; per-station obscurations for 8 of 9 stations deliberately deferred to evaluation-time NASA pulls rather than committed in-document.

proposed_files.description: Canonical immutable predictions JSON. Decider commits, then operator runs `ots stamp` on the file's bytes. After OTS anchoring this file MUST NOT be modified.

_meta.title: Frozen Prospective Predictions — Total Solar Eclipse 2026 August 12
_meta.registration_purpose: Prospective registration of three-bucket predictions (standard physics, dome-geometry-forced, symmetric falsification) for the 2026 Aug 12 total solar eclipse, intended for OpenTimestamps anchoring before the event so that post-event observations can be compared to pre-registered claims.
_meta.immutability_notice: After OTS-anchoring this file MUST NOT be modified. Any updates to this analysis go in a new dated file and are anchored separately. Errata are published as separate dated addendum files; this file's claims stand as registered.
_meta.verification_instructions: (1) Verify the OTS proof: `ots verify eclipse-2026-08-12-predictions.json.ots` against this file's bytes. (2) Cross-check standard-physics numbers against the cited NASA Besselian-elements page (eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/SEbeselm/SEbeselm2001/SE2026Aug12Tbeselm.html) and path-table page (eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/SEpath/SEpath2001/SE2026Aug12Tpath.html). (3) Cross-check dome parameters by checking out the dome repo at the pinned dome_repo_commit_sha and inspecting api/current/formula_runtime.json (FORM-V51-001 H(r), FORM-V51-005 FSF), and predictions.html (per-station coverage/FSF table). (4) Cross-check our per-station distance computations by retrieving review_repo at review_repo_commit_sha and reading data/sections.json part4 element id="p4-427-station-coverage".
_meta.audience: A third party reading this file plus the cited public sources should be able to verify each claim independently.

context.what_this_is: Three buckets of prospective predictions for the 2026 Aug 12 total solar eclipse, registered before the event and anchored with OpenTimestamps so that no party — including us — can rewrite the predictions after observation. This file is the canary-by-example: a textbook example of proper prospective prediction registration (predictions in their own immutable doc, OTS-anchored separately from observations).
context.why_three_buckets: Bucket 1 records what standard physics predicts. Bucket 2 records what the dome model — taken on its own published parameters at the pinned dome SHA — must predict. Bucket 3 records the symmetric falsification criteria for both models AND for our own analysis. After the event, observations are compared to the predictions in all three buckets.
context.scope_limitation: This is a high-confidence-only registration. Peripheral or weakly-endorsed claims have been excluded deliberately — over-commitment in a frozen document hurts more than under-commitment. Where per-station NASA values are not directly cited herein (e.g., per-station obscuration), the prediction defers to NASA Besselian-element pulls at evaluation time rather than committing to in-document values that we have not independently fetched and cited.

event.name: Total Solar Eclipse 2026 August 12
event.nasa_authoritative_source: F. Espenak, NASA GSFC Planetary Systems Laboratory

bucket_1_standard_physics.description: Predictions that follow from standard astronomical physics (distant Sun and Moon, spherical Earth, Besselian-elements geometry) and the Chapman-Sq ionospheric mechanism (Chapman 1919; Yamazaki & Maute 2017; Kim & Chang 2018) for the magnetic-field component.

geometric_predictions.claim: Path of totality maximum width at greatest eclipse
geometric_predictions.confidence_bound: ±5 km (NASA Besselian-elements precision; rounded to whole-km for the central-line catalog)
geometric_predictions.source: NASA SE2026Aug12T path table at greatest eclipse (17:45:53.8 UT)

geometric_predictions.claim: Maximum central-line totality duration at greatest eclipse
geometric_predictions.source: NASA SE2026Aug12T path table greatest-duration entry (17:44:42 UT)
geometric_predictions.review_cross_ref: data/sections.json part4 §4.2.7 (138.2 s figure used in 4.2.7 trilemma argument)

geometric_predictions.claim: Sun apparent semi-diameter at greatest eclipse
Moon apparent semi-diameter at greatest eclipse
Hartland (51.00° N, 4.48° W) maximum sun obscuration during partial-eclipse window
geometric_predictions.confidence_bound: ±2 percentage points (rounding plus local-circumstances precision; Hartland is well off the central line, so partial-coverage peak is the relevant metric)
geometric_predictions.source: NASA SE2026Aug12T Besselian elements + local-circumstances calculation for Hartland coordinates; figure used in our review §4.2.7
geometric_predictions.review_cross_ref: data/sections.json part4 §4.2.7 ('at Hartland, partial coverage lasts roughly 2 h 45 min and peaks at ≈ 88 % obscuration')
geometric_predictions.note_for_other_stations: Per-station obscuration percentages for the other 8 dome-cited stations are not committed here in this frozen file. We register Hartland 88% as the only per-station NASA-anchored obscuration value because it is the one explicitly documented in our review at the pinned commit. Per-station obscurations for the other stations should be pulled from NASA local-circumstances at evaluation time (we register the methodology, not a hand-computed table).

magnetic_predictions.claim: During the partial-eclipse window over north-western Europe, INTERMAGNET stations on the standard-physics path of partial coverage record a magnetic-field perturbation pattern consistent with the Chapman-Sq ionospheric mechanism: peak amplitude of order single-digit-to-low-double-digit nT (commonly 5–25 nT) at high-coverage stations under geomagnetically-quiet conditions (Kp < 2), with the perturbation tracking the apparent-shadow geometry over a window of order 60–180 minutes (E-region recovery timescale).
magnetic_predictions.amplitude_unit: nT (peak deviation from quiet-day baseline, station-specific)
amplitude_caveats: Range derived from the literature-documented Chapman-Sq amplitude expectation for high-coverage stations during quiet conditions; cited in our review §4.2.3.

Exact per-station central values require running a Chapman-Sq / Yamazaki-Maute Sq-conductivity model with E-region electron densities (ionosonde / GPS-TEC / climatology) for the eclipse window, which is not done in-line in this frozen file.
Disturbed conditions (Kp ≥ 2) inflate the noise floor and may obscure or distort the eclipse signal; this is well-known and is not by itself a falsification of either model.
scientific_sources: Chapman, S. (1919). The solar and lunar diurnal variations of terrestrial magnetism. Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218: 1–118.

Yamazaki, Y., & Maute, A. (2017). Sq and EEJ — A review on the daily variation of the geomagnetic field caused by ionospheric dynamo currents. Space Science Reviews 206: 299–405. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11214-016-0282-z
Kim, V. P., & Chang, H. Y. (2018). Geomagnetic effects of solar eclipses studied with a 39-eclipse 1991-2016 INTERMAGNET ensemble. Adv. Space Res. 61: 2040–2049.
magnetic_predictions.review_cross_ref: data/wins.json WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble) and §4.2.3 (Chapman-Sq amplitude window framing)

bucket_2_dome_geometry_forced.description: Predictions that follow from the dome model's own published parameters at the pinned dome SHA, taken on its own terms. The dome publishes specific altitudes for Sun (5,733 km) and Moon (2,534 km), specific firmament/refraction formulas (FORM-V51-001, FORM-V51-002), specific eclipse-amplitude formula (ΔB = baseline × coverage × FSF), and a specific 9-station eclipse table. The predictions in this bucket are forced by those parameters once the standard NASA angular sizes and observed magnitudes are accepted as inputs.

dome_params_pinned.fsf_table_observed_match: FSF = tan(lat)/tan(40°) reproduces published per-station FSFs at Pearson r ≈ 0.9999 (review §4.2.4)

geometric_predictions.claim: Totality (umbra) circle diameter at disc level, derived from h_Sun = 5,733 km, h_Moon = 2,534 km, NASA Sun semi-diameter 15'47.0" and Moon semi-diameter 16'16.9"
geometric_predictions.derivation: R_Sun = h_Sun · tan(α_Sun) = 5,733 × tan(0.26306°) = 26.32 km; R_Moon = h_Moon · tan(α_Moon) = 2,534 × tan(0.27136°) = 12.00 km; D = h_Sun − h_Moon = 3,199 km; umbra radius at disc R_u = R_Moon − (R_Sun − R_Moon) · h_Moon / D = 12.00 − 14.32 × 2,534 / 3,199 = 0.658 km. Totality diameter = 2 R_u = 1.317 km.
geometric_predictions.confidence_bound: ±0.03 km (rounding of input semi-diameters; result is dominated by the h_Sun − h_Moon difference and small changes in either altitude swing R_u by ~0.05–0.1 km)
geometric_predictions.review_cross_ref: data/sections.json part4 §4.2.7 ('a totality circle roughly 1.3 km in diameter')

geometric_predictions.claim: Penumbra (any-partial-coverage) ring radius at disc level, same dome parameters
geometric_predictions.derivation: R_p = R_Moon + (R_Sun + R_Moon) · h_Moon / D = 12.00 + 38.32 × 2,534 / 3,199 = 42.36 km. Penumbra band width = 2 R_p ≈ 84.7 km.
geometric_predictions.confidence_bound: ±2 km (input rounding + parallel-ray approximation of the dome's own eclipse-page geometry)

geometric_predictions.claim: Single-station totality duration under dome geometry, lower and upper bounds
geometric_predictions.derivation: Lower bound: Moon transits the 1.317 km totality strip at the rim-orbit upper bound 87 km/min (24-h circuit of 20,015 km disc rim) → 1.317 / 87 × 60 ≈ 0.9 s. Upper bound: at the Moon's actual observed angular speed (~0.25°/min at h_Moon = 2,534 km, equivalent to ~11 km/min ground speed) → 1.317 / 11 × 60 ≈ 7 s. Mid-disc charity at 25 km/min gives ~3 s.
geometric_predictions.review_cross_ref: data/sections.json part4 §4.2.7 ('lengthening to about 3 seconds at 25 km/min … only ~7 seconds … still close to 20× short of the observed 138-second maximum')

geometric_predictions.claim: Number of dome's own 9 published eclipse stations sitting inside the dome-geometry penumbra band (R_disc/90 charitable AE-from-N projection)
geometric_predictions.robustness_check_stricter_AE_convention: Under the stricter AE-from-S convention (R_disc/180), every per-station distance halves but the closest station (Ebro at 222 km) still sits 5.3× R_p outside the band — 0/9 headline survives.
geometric_predictions.review_cross_ref: data/sections.json part4 §4.2.7a 'Per-station distance from the projected umbra path' table

magnetic_predictions.claim: Per-station ΔB predicted by dome's published formula ΔB = baseline × coverage × FSF, using BOU 2017 baseline = -10.9 nT and dome's own published per-station coverage and FSF values
magnetic_predictions.baseline_source: Boulder (BOU) 2017 eclipse anchor used by the dome for the V51.1 formula; review WIN-010

magnetic_predictions.confidence_bound: Per-station central values are exact-arithmetic from dome-published numerical inputs (no scientific uncertainty added); the range across stations is -5.04 nT (Canary) to -12.83 nT (Hartland) under the BOU-baseline convention.
magnetic_predictions.alternate_baseline_note: Our review §4.2.3 documents that the dome publishes a second baseline (W004) that yields larger per-station amplitudes (range -10 to -26 nT, with Hartland at -26.2 nT). The dual-baseline structure is itself a §4.2.3 issue; we register the BOU-baseline numbers here because BOU is the dome-cited 2017 anchor used in WIN-010.
magnetic_predictions.internal_inconsistency_with_dome_PRED_09: The dome's PRED-095 / PRED-TIER-1 (registered 2026-04-04) specify -17 to -21 nT at >80% coverage stations. Computing the dome's BOU-baseline formula at the dome's own published coverage and FSF values yields -10.66 nT (Ebro), -12.83 nT (Hartland), -10.10 nT (Coimbra), -9.82 nT (San Pablo) for the four >80% coverage stations — none in the -17 to -21 nT range. The internal mismatch between dome formula and dome PRED amplitude is documented in our review §4.2.3.
magnetic_predictions.review_cross_ref: data/sections.json part4 §4.2.7a station table (dome-published coverage and FSF) + §4.2.3 (dual-baseline framing) + data/predictions.json PRED-095, PRED-TIER-1 (amplitude window)

magnetic_predictions.claim: Geometric closure: under dome geometry, 8 of 9 dome-cited stations cannot record a meaningful eclipse magnetic signal because they sit outside the dome-geometry penumbra band, even though the dome's own ΔB formula assigns them a non-zero magnitude.
magnetic_predictions.value: 0 of 9 inside the 84.7-km penumbra band under R_disc/90 charity; ≤ 1 of 9 inside under stricter R_disc/180 convention
magnetic_predictions.implication: If the dome's V51 occultation geometry is correct, 8 or 9 of the 9 named stations must record exactly zero signal during the eclipse window. The dome's own ΔB formula assigning -5 to -13 nT to all 9 is therefore incompatible with V51 geometry.

bucket_3_falsification_criteria.description: Symmetric falsification criteria. After the 2026 Aug 12 event, the observations on the published surface (NASA path data, INTERMAGNET 1-minute traces, Sun obscuration percentages at named stations) determine which model — if either — survives. The criteria below are pre-registered. We commit to honoring them whether the verdict favors us or not.

criteria: OBS-D-01: Observed central-line path-of-totality width at greatest eclipse matches NASA's 294 km prediction within ±20 km tolerance, AND maximum totality duration matches NASA's 138.2 s within ±10 s tolerance. Standard physics is corroborated; dome-geometry-forced 1.3-km totality strip and ≤ 7-s single-station duration is falsified.

OBS-D-02: Magnetic field at the 9 dome-cited stations during the eclipse window is incompatible with ΔB = -10.9 × coverage × FSF (BOU baseline) within station-specific noise floor. If observed amplitudes deviate from dome-formula predictions by > 2× the per-station noise floor at a majority of stations, the dome formula is falsified.

OBS-D-03: Observed magnetic-signal pattern across the 9 stations tracks NASA per-station obscuration (which is set by globe-physics path-of-totality geometry) rather than dome's published per-station 'coverage' assignments. This is the §4.2.7 trilemma's 'Borrowed' branch made empirical: globe physics chooses where the signal appears, the dome's per-station coverage table is a parasitic relabeling.

OBS-D-04: At least one of the 9 dome-cited stations is observed to be inside the standard-physics path of partial eclipse (i.e., NASA-predicted obscuration > 0%) AND records the Chapman-Sq amplitude expected for that obscuration during quiet conditions. This corroborates standard physics for at least one named dome station and falsifies any dome reading in which that station was supposed to record a different signal.

OBS-S-01: Observed path-of-totality width at greatest eclipse is ~1 km (consistent with dome geometry) rather than ~294 km (consistent with NASA Besselian elements). Dome geometry is corroborated; standard physics is falsified. Tolerance: any observation < 10 km wide path-of-totality on the central-line is incompatible with NASA's 294 km prediction.

OBS-S-02: At all 9 dome-cited stations, NO magnetic dip is observed during the eclipse window despite NASA-predicted high obscuration at the Iberian-Atlantic stations and quiet conditions (Kp < 2). Specifically, peak ΔB at any high-obscuration (NASA obscuration > 50%) station is < 1 nT during the eclipse window. This would falsify Chapman-Sq.

OBS-S-03: Observed per-station Sun obscuration percentages diverge from NASA Besselian-elements predictions by > 10 percentage points at a majority of the 9 stations. This would falsify the standard-physics geometric prediction for the event.

OBS-O-01: BOTH standard-physics predictions AND dome-geometry-forced predictions are corroborated by observation. This is impossible if the §4.2.7 trilemma is correct (the trilemma asserts the two cannot both hold for a single event). If both bucket 1 and bucket 2 predictions hold simultaneously, our analysis of the dome's geometric closure is wrong somewhere, and we must publicly retract the trilemma framing in §4.2.7.

OBS-O-02: A measurement methodology not anticipated in this file produces an observation that cannot be cleanly interpreted under either Chapman-Sq or dome-geometry. Examples: a hitherto-unmodeled signal mode (e.g., a non-magnetic, non-gravitational coupling between obscuration and station readouts that neither framework predicts); a measurement artifact at a previously-uncharacterized scale that contaminates both models' signals identically. In such a case our trilemma framing remains valid but our claim that the August 12 2026 event is a clean test of either model is weakened, and we publicly note the limit.

OBS-O-03: If observed per-station ΔB at the 9 dome-cited stations matches dome's BOU-baseline formula predictions (B2-M-001 per_station central values) within station-specific noise floors AND no dip is observed at any non-dome-cited high-obscuration INTERMAGNET station (e.g., other European observatories not on the dome's 9-station list but at comparable obscuration) — that pattern is unexplained under either standard physics (which predicts Chapman-Sq at all high-obscuration stations regardless of dome's selection) or dome geometry (which puts every dome-cited station outside the penumbra band). Our analysis would need to be expanded to account for whatever mechanism privileges the 9 dome-cited stations specifically.

rows: B1-G-001 | B1 standard physics | Path width 294.0 km ± 5 km
B1-G-002 | B1 standard physics | Max duration 138.2 s ± 0.1 s
B1-G-005 | B1 standard physics | Hartland peak obscuration 88% ± 2 pp
B1-M-001 | B1 standard physics | Chapman-Sq amplitudes 5–25 nT high-coverage stations, quiet conditions
B2-G-001 | B2 dome geometry | Totality diameter 1.317 km ± 0.03 km
B2-G-002 | B2 dome geometry | Penumbra radius 42.4 km ± 2 km
B2-G-003 | B2 dome geometry | Single-station totality duration 0.9–7 s
B2-G-004 | B2 dome geometry | 0/9 dome stations inside dome-geometry penumbra band
B2-M-001 | B2 dome geometry | Per-station ΔB from dome formula -5 to -13 nT (BOU baseline)
B2-M-002 | B2 dome geometry | 8/9 dome stations should record ZERO during eclipse window
B3-F-DOME | B3 falsification | Dome model falsified by OBS-D-01..04
B3-F-STDPHYS | B3 falsification | Standard physics falsified by OBS-S-01..03
B3-F-OURS | B3 falsification | Our analysis falsified by OBS-O-01..03

procedure: 1. After 2026-08-12, retrieve INTERMAGNET 1-minute data for all 9 dome-cited stations covering the eclipse window (typically ±3 hours from local maximum coverage).
2. Compute per-station peak ΔB after subtracting a quiet-day baseline (standard preprocessing).
3. Retrieve NASA per-station local-circumstances obscuration for each of the 9 stations (eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov local-circumstances calculator at the station's WGS84 coordinates).
4. Compare observed ΔB per station to (a) Bucket 2 per-station central values (dome BOU formula), (b) Bucket 1 Chapman-Sq amplitude window scaled to per-station obscuration.
5. Test each falsification criterion in Bucket 3. Record which criteria (if any) fired.
6. Publish results in a separate dated file under predictions/eclipse-2026-08-12-results.json (and OTS-anchor it independently from this file). Do NOT modify this file.
post_event_protocol.honesty_clause: If observation falsifies our analysis (Bucket 3 OBS-O criteria), we publicly retract the relevant analysis in our review and update §4.2.7 / §4.2.7a accordingly. The frozen file remains as the registered prediction; the retraction is published in a separate dated addendum.

proposed_files.description: Optional human-readable Markdown mirror of the JSON, same content, no extra claims. Provided for readers who don't want to parse JSON. Mirror is NOT independently OTS-anchored — the JSON is canonical.
proposed_files.contents_note: Decider may render the JSON to Markdown using a deterministic transform OR omit this file. The JSON file is the canonical artifact.

decider_integration_steps: 2. Write `predictions/eclipse-2026-08-12-predictions.json` with EXACT bytes of proposed_files[0].contents (JSON.stringify with 2-space indent and trailing newline). NO modifications, NO timestamps regenerated, NO whitespace tweaks — bytes are about to be hashed.
4. Run tests/run.sh — should pass without modifications since this is a new file, not an edit to existing data files.
5. Commit with message `EXP-283: frozen prospective predictions for 2026-08-12 total solar eclipse`. Push.
6. Route to curmudgeon with HNOTE-CURMUDGEON-FROZEN-PRED-REVIEW-RUBRIC-001 dual-pass rubric. DO NOT proceed to OTS-anchoring until curmudgeon dual-pass clears AND operator approves.
7. After curmudgeon clears + operator approves, operator runs `ots stamp predictions/eclipse-2026-08-12-predictions.json` and commits the resulting `.ots` file.
8. Update tracker entry for EXP-283: status=integrated, integrated_at=<now>, integrated_by=decider (per HNOTE-OPERATOR-DECIDER-EXPANSION-TRACKER-SYNC-001 direction A).
tests_required: tests/run.sh passes without modification (new file only — no DATA blob changes).
node -e "JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('predictions/eclipse-2026-08-12-predictions.json','utf8'))" returns no error (the curmudgeon and decider should both run this before OTS anchoring).

anticipated_objections.objection: Locking the file forever — a typo or unit error becomes part of the proof permanently.
anticipated_objections.response: Mitigated by curmudgeon dual-pass review (per HNOTE-CURMUDGEON-FROZEN-PRED-REVIEW-RUBRIC-001) before OTS-anchoring, AND by deliberate exclusion of peripheral claims. Per-station obscurations for 8 of 9 stations are NOT committed in-document precisely because we have not independently fetched them; the methodology is committed instead. Bucket 3 OBS-O-02 explicitly registers the possibility that an unanticipated measurement methodology produces an unexpected result — we acknowledge our analysis may need to be expanded post-event.

anticipated_objections.objection: Hostage to dome's params — the dome author may change V51.1 between freeze and 2026-08-12 to escape the trilemma.
anticipated_objections.response: The dome SHA (c3b9f56fe622f6d3162a03671e797fe74fa6859a, 2026-05-02T00:03:03Z) is pinned in _meta. The frozen file remains correct as a snapshot at the pinned SHA, even if the dome later moves. Bucket 2 explicitly documents the dome parameters at the pinned SHA, so a dome-side parameter change after freeze does not invalidate the registered prediction; it simply means the dome at the new SHA is a different model that we have not separately analyzed.

anticipated_objections.objection: Tipping our hand on the canary — publishing a textbook example of proper OTS may make the dome author either fix his methodology (canary fires) or dig in (canary doesn't fire but the educational artifact still exists).
anticipated_objections.response: Operator accepts this tradeoff per the HNOTE. Either outcome is informative: a fix is the strongest signal we have that the dome author is reading our review and reactively patching his methodology; non-fix leaves us with the educational artifact and a longer-term track record.

anticipated_objections.objection: Hartland 88% obscuration is the only per-station NASA value we commit to — the other 8 stations are deferred. A hostile reader could attack this as picking-and-choosing.
anticipated_objections.response: We register the methodology for all 9 stations (NASA local-circumstances pulls at evaluation time) and the per-station value for the only one our review at the pinned commit explicitly cites. This is the high-confidence-only constraint — committing to per-station NASA values we have not independently fetched would be over-commitment of exactly the kind this frozen file is designed to avoid. Bucket 3 OBS-D-03 (signal pattern tracks NASA per-station obscuration) is the testable claim; the per-station obscurations are evaluation-time inputs, not pre-committed predictions.

anticipated_objections.objection: PRED-095 / PRED-TIER-1 amplitude window (-17 to -21 nT) is dome's stated prediction; our Bucket 2 BOU-baseline computation gives -5 to -13 nT. A defender could argue we're using the wrong baseline.
anticipated_objections.response: Bucket 2 documents BOTH baselines (BOU and W004) and explicitly notes the internal inconsistency between dome's published formula and dome's PRED-095 amplitude window. The dual-baseline structure is itself a §4.2.3 issue and is registered in the frozen file (under B2-M-001 alternate_baseline_note). Whichever baseline post-event observation favors, the dome's other baseline is falsified by the same data — the dual-baseline construction was the §4.2.3 hedge, and it dissolves when forced to commit to one number per station.

newsworthy: Frozen prospective predictions registered for August 12, 2026 total solar eclipse — dome geometry vs standard physics test.
